title: Daily Routine
description: Describe your daily life in Vietnamese — from waking up to going to bed. Learn the everyday verbs your family uses and how Southern Vietnamese daily rhythms shape the language.

sections.content:
Daily routine vocabulary is some of the most practical Vietnamese you can learn. These are the words your family uses every single day — 'Dậy đi con!' (Wake up!), 'Ăn cơm chưa?' (Have you eaten yet?), 'Đi ngủ đi!' (Go to sleep!).

Vietnamese daily life is family-centered. Meals are shared, not individual. Someone is always 'nấu cơm' (cooking rice = preparing dinner). And 'Ăn cơm chưa?' is less a question about food and more a greeting — the Vietnamese way of asking 'How are you?'

items.notes: 'Dậy đi!' is the short command form — what your parents yelled every morning. 'Dậy sớm' = wake up early. Southern Vietnamese families often wake up very early (5-6 AM).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Má kêu thức dậy từ sáu giờ mà tui ngủ nướng.
exampleSentence.english: Mom called me to wake up at six but I slept in.

items.notes: A wonderfully Southern expression. 'Nướng' literally means 'to grill/roast' — so 'ngủ nướng' is like 'roasting in sleep'. The Northern equivalent is 'ngủ dậy muộn'. Very casual and playful.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cuối tuần cho tui ngủ nướng chút đi.
exampleSentence.english: Let me sleep in a bit on the weekend.

items.notes: 'Đánh' literally means 'to hit/strike'. 'Đánh răng' = strike teeth = brush teeth. Vietnamese is wonderfully literal sometimes. Southern pronunciation of 'răng': /ɹaŋ/.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Đánh răng rồi mới được ăn sáng.
exampleSentence.english: Brush your teeth first, then you can eat breakfast.

items.notes: 'Tắm' covers both bathing and showering. 'Tắm rửa' = wash up (more thorough). In Vietnam, many people shower twice a day because of the heat. 'Đi tắm đi!' = Go shower!

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Nóng quá, tui đi tắm trước nha.
exampleSentence.english: It's so hot — I'm going to shower first, okay?

items.notes: 'Ăn' = eat, 'sáng' = morning. Similarly: 'ăn trưa' (lunch), 'ăn chiều/ăn tối' (dinner). Vietnamese breakfast is often savory — phở, bánh mì, cháo (congee), xôi (sticky rice).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Sáng nay ăn sáng gì? Có bánh mì không?
exampleSentence.english: What's for breakfast today? Is there bánh mì?

items.notes: 'Đi' = to go + activity is a common pattern: 'đi làm' (go to work), 'đi học' (go to school), 'đi chợ' (go to the market), 'đi chơi' (go out for fun).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ba đi làm rồi, tối mới về.
exampleSentence.english: Dad already went to work — he won't be back until tonight.

items.english: to go to school, to study
items.notes: 'Đi học' = go to school. 'Học bài' = study/do homework. Vietnamese parents famously emphasize education: 'Học đi!' (Go study!) is heard in every household.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Con đi học về rồi thì học bài liền nha.
exampleSentence.english: After you get home from school, do your homework right away.

items.notes: 'Về' = to return. 'Về nhà' = return home. 'Về Việt Nam' = go back to Vietnam. 'Bao giờ về?' = When are you coming home? (Every Vietnamese parent's question.)

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Về nhà sớm nha con, đừng đi chơi khuya.
exampleSentence.english: Come home early, okay? Don't stay out late.

items.english: to cook rice / to prepare a meal
items.notes: 'Nấu cơm' literally means 'cook rice' but really means 'prepare dinner/a meal'. Rice is so central to Vietnamese meals that cooking rice = cooking the whole meal. 'Nấu ăn' = to cook (general).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Hôm nay ai nấu cơm? Má đi làm về trễ.
exampleSentence.english: Who's cooking today? Mom is coming home from work late.

items.english: to eat a meal (literally: eat rice)
items.notes: 'Ăn cơm chưa?' (Have you eaten yet?) is the quintessential Vietnamese greeting — it shows care by asking about your most basic need. The answer is 'Ăn rồi' (already ate) or 'Chưa' (not yet).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ăn cơm chưa con? Vô ăn đi!
exampleSentence.english: Have you eaten yet? Come eat!

items.notes: 'Chén' is the Southern word for bowl/dish. Northern speakers say 'rửa bát'. 'Rửa chén' is one of those markers that instantly identifies Southern speech.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ăn xong rồi đi rửa chén đi con.
exampleSentence.english: You're done eating — go wash the dishes.

items.notes: 'Coi' is Southern for 'to watch/look'. Northern uses 'xem'. 'Ti vi' comes from French 'télévision'. So 'coi ti vi' = Southern, 'xem ti vi' = Northern. 'Coi phim' = watch a movie.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ba coi ti vi hoài, không chịu đi ngủ.
exampleSentence.english: Dad keeps watching TV and won't go to sleep.

items.notes: 'Đi ngủ đi!' = Go to sleep! 'Ngủ sớm' = sleep early. 'Ngủ trưa' = afternoon nap (common in Vietnam due to the heat). 'Buồn ngủ' = sleepy.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Khuya rồi, đi ngủ đi con.
exampleSentence.english: It's late — go to sleep, child.

items.english: to go to the market / go grocery shopping
items.notes: In Vietnam, 'đi chợ' means going to an open-air market, not a supermarket. Even in the diaspora, Vietnamese families may still say 'đi chợ' for any grocery shopping. The market is a social hub.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Sáng nào má cũng đi chợ mua đồ nấu ăn.
exampleSentence.english: Every morning Mom goes to the market to buy ingredients for cooking.

sections.content:
Vietnamese daily life revolves around family meals. 'Ăn cơm chưa?' (Have you eaten yet?) isn't really about food — it's the Vietnamese 'How are you?', expressing care and connection. Refusing food in a Vietnamese home is borderline offensive — even if you're full, at least try a little.

The rhythm of a Southern Vietnamese day typically follows:
- **5-6 AM:** Wake up (dậy sớm), especially for the older generation
- **Morning:** Breakfast outside (phở, bánh mì, cà phê sữa đá)
- **Midday:** Lunch at home, then a nap (ngủ trưa) during the hottest hours
- **Afternoon:** Work or errands, then evening market
- **Evening:** Family dinner together (the most important meal), then TV or chatting
- **9-10 PM:** Sleep (earlier than Western habits)

For heritage speakers, understanding this daily rhythm helps explain why your grandparents wake up at 5 AM and why dinner together is non-negotiable.

**The 'đi + activity' pattern:**

Vietnamese uses 'đi' (to go) before activities to indicate going somewhere to do them:

| Vietnamese | Literal | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| đi làm | go + work | go to work |
| đi học | go + study | go to school |
| đi chợ | go + market | go grocery shopping |
| đi chơi | go + play | go out for fun |
| đi ngủ | go + sleep | go to bed |
| đi tắm | go + bathe | go take a shower |

This pattern is extremely productive — you can combine 'đi' with almost any activity verb.

exercises.question: What does 'Ăn cơm chưa?' really mean in Vietnamese culture?
options: It's a greeting meaning 'How are you?' / showing care
exercises.correctAnswer: It's a greeting meaning 'How are you?' / showing care
exercises.explanation: 'Ăn cơm chưa?' (Have you eaten yet?) is the quintessential Vietnamese greeting. It's not really asking about food — it's showing care and concern, equivalent to 'How are you?'

exercises.question: What is 'ngủ nướng' and which dialect is it from?
exercises.explanation: 'Ngủ nướng' is a playful Southern expression meaning to sleep in. 'Nướng' literally means 'to grill/roast' — so you're 'roasting in bed'. It's distinctly Southern Vietnamese.

exercises.question: What is the Southern Vietnamese way to say 'wash dishes'?
exercises.explanation: 'Rửa chén' is Southern; 'rửa bát' is Northern. 'Chén' vs 'bát' (both meaning bowl) is one of the classic North-South vocabulary differences.

exercises.question: Complete what a Vietnamese parent says at night:
exercises.english: It's late — go to sleep, child.
exercises.explanation: 'Đi ngủ đi!' = Go to sleep! Following the 'đi + activity' pattern. The final 'đi' is a command particle that softens the order.

exercises.question: Type 'Have you eaten yet?' in Vietnamese (the classic greeting):
exercises.explanation: 'Ăn cơm chưa?' literally means 'Eat rice yet?' — the most iconic Vietnamese greeting-as-care expression. 'Chưa' = yet/not yet.
